stories.title: Sinner Dismantles Djokovic 6-4, 6-4, 6-4 — Into Final as Defending Champion

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
Jannik Sinner is going to the Wimbledon final.

6-4, 6-4, 6-4 over Djokovic. Zero break points faced. 88% first-serve points won. 16 aces.

The defending champion looks like the best player on the planet. #Wimbledon #Tennis #TennisFanrecap

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
If Sinner wins Sunday, he'll be the first man to defend the Wimbledon title since Roger Federer in 2009.

He wasn't broken once against Djokovic. The best player in the world is playing his best tennis. #Wimbledon #Sinner #TennisFanrecap

facebook.long_form:
Jannik Sinner is heading to the Wimbledon final, and his performance against Novak Djokovic yesterday was as close to perfection as it gets.

The final score — 6-4, 6-4, 6-4 — doesn't fully capture how dominant this was. Sinner did not face a single break point the entire match. He won 88% of his first-serve points. He hit 16 aces. At no point did Djokovic, playing through a left ankle and calf injury from his grueling 5h15m quarterfinal epic, find a crack in the armor.

Djokovic came to Wimbledon chasing his 25th Grand Slam title and his eighth Wimbledon crown. Sinner denied him at the penultimate step, continuing what has been the most dominant run through a Wimbledon draw in recent memory — dropping just two sets all tournament, both in a five-set opening-round match against Kecmanovic.

On Sunday, Sinner gets a chance to do something that hasn't been done in 17 years: defend the Wimbledon title. The last man to manage it was Roger Federer in 2009.

His opponent will be Alexander Zverev.

Who do you think wins Sunday? 🎾
facebook.image_caption: Sinner. Zero break points. 88% first-serve points won. 16 aces. The defending champion is locked in. 🎾 #Wimbledon

stories.title: Zverev Ends "Fery-Tale" 7-6(0), 6-2, 6-4 — Into First Wimbledon Final

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text:
Arthur Fery entered Wimbledon ranked No. 114. He leaves as world No. 36, British No. 1, with ~$1.2M in prize money — more than his entire career earnings before this tournament.

The Fery-tale is over. The story is just beginning. #Wimbledon #TennisFanrecap

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text:
Zverev into his first Wimbledon final — 7-6(0), 6-2, 6-4 vs Fery.

He won Roland Garros in May. A win Sunday would be the Channel Slam — only Laver, Borg, Federer, Nadal, and Djokovic have done it. #Wimbledon #Zverev #TennisFanrecap

facebook.long_form:
The Fery-tale had to end somewhere. On Friday at Wimbledon, it ended against the formidable Alexander Zverev — 7-6(0), 6-2, 6-4.

But let's be honest: the story of Arthur Fery isn't about the loss. It's about everything that happened before it.

Arthur Fery arrived at Wimbledon as a 23-year-old British wild card ranked No. 114 in the world. Two weeks later, he departs as world No. 36 — a career high — and the new British No. 1, displacing Cam Norrie. The prize money from this run? Approximately $1.2 million. His entire career earnings before Wimbledon? $884,000. In two weeks, he more than doubled everything he'd earned in his professional career.

He also did something only one other man in the Open Era has done: reach the Wimbledon men's semifinals as a wild card. The first was Goran Ivanisevic in 2001. Ivanisevic won the title that year.

As for Zverev — he's into his first Wimbledon final. He arrived as the reigning Roland Garros champion. A win on Sunday would complete the Channel Slam (winning Roland Garros and Wimbledon in the same year) — a feat achieved only by Rod Laver, Björn Borg, Roger Federer, Rafael Nadal, and Novak Djokovic. Zverev has a chance to write himself into that company.

The Fery story is extraordinary. But Zverev's moment is arriving too.

What was your favorite Fery moment from this tournament? 🎾
facebook.image_caption: The Fery-tale is over — but No. 114 to No. 36 in two weeks is a career transformed. And Zverev's Channel Slam bid is alive. 🎾 #Wimbledon

stories.title: Women's Final Preview — Muchova vs. Noskova, All-Czech Wimbledon Showdown

x_posts.label: Post 3-1
x_posts.text:
Today's Wimbledon women's final is unlike anything the sport has seen.

No. 10 Karolina Muchova vs. No. 9 Linda Noskova — first all-Czech women's Grand Slam final ever.

They're both from Přerov. Population: 43,000. #Wimbledon #WTA #TennisFanrecap

x_posts.label: Post 3-2
x_posts.text:
Muchova vs. Noskova starts not before 4 PM BST / 11 AM ET on Centre Court.

First same-nationality women's GS final since Venus vs. Serena at Wimbledon 2009.

One of them wins their first Grand Slam title today. #Wimbledon #WTA #TennisFanrecap

facebook.long_form:
Something genuinely historic is happening at Wimbledon today, and it involves a small city in the Czech Republic called Přerov.

Population: 43,000. Current Grand Slam finalists produced: two.

Karolina Muchova, 29, and Linda Noskova, 21, are both from Přerov — and today they face each other in the Wimbledon women's final on Centre Court. This is the first all-Czech women's Grand Slam final in history. It's also the first women's Grand Slam final between two players of the same nationality since Venus and Serena Williams met at Wimbledon in 2009.

There is no precedent for what they're playing for. The title is guaranteed to go to a first-time Wimbledon champion.

Muchova is 29 and in her second Grand Slam final (she reached the 2023 French Open final). She's the oldest first-time Wimbledon finalist since Nathalie Tauziat in 1998. She got here by saving a match point in the tiebreak against Coco Gauff in a semifinal that had the entire tennis world on edge.

Noskova is 21 and playing in her first Grand Slam final. She dismantled Marta Kostyuk 6-4, 6-4 in just 79 minutes — barely breaking a sweat in the semifinal.

Czech women have won Wimbledon in 2023 (Marketa Vondrousova) and 2024 (Barbora Krejcikova). If either Muchova or Noskova wins today, that's three of the last four Wimbledon women's titles going to the Czech Republic.

Match begins not before 4:00 PM BST / 11:00 AM ET on Centre Court, after the men's doubles final.

Who are you rooting for today? Muchova or Noskova? 🇨🇿🎾
facebook.image_caption: Both from the same Czech city of 43,000. Both in the Wimbledon final. History happening today. 🇨🇿🎾 #Wimbledon

stories.title: Men's Final Preview — Sinner vs. Zverev, Sunday on Centre Court

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
Sunday's Wimbledon men's final: No. 1 Sinner vs. No. 2 Zverev.

Not before 4 PM BST / 11 AM ET on Centre Court.

First time ATP No. 1 and No. 2 have met in the Wimbledon final in the Open Era. #Wimbledon #Tennis #TennisFanrecap

facebook.long_form:
The Wimbledon men's final is set.

No. 1 Jannik Sinner (Italy) vs. No. 2 Alexander Zverev (Germany). Sunday, July 12. Centre Court. Not before 4:00 PM BST / 11:00 AM ET.

This is the first time the ATP No. 1 and No. 2 seeds have met in the Wimbledon men's final in the Open Era. Two of the very best players in the world, at the peak of their powers, on the biggest stage in the sport.

For Sinner, it's a chance to defend the Wimbledon title he won last year — something no man has managed since Roger Federer did it in 2008 and 2009. He hasn't been broken all tournament.

For Zverev, it's the chance to complete the Channel Slam — winning the French Open and Wimbledon in the same year. The last man to do it was Federer in 2009. Before him: Djokovic, Nadal, Borg, and Rod Laver. Zverev won Roland Garros in May. He's one match from completing something only five men have ever done.

They've met 14 times before. They know each other extremely well. But Wimbledon grass is different, and both have had exceptional draws.

Set your alarms for Sunday. This one's going to be special.

What's your prediction — Sinner defends, or Zverev writes history? 🎾
facebook.image_caption: Sinner or Zverev. Sunday's Wimbledon final is set — and it doesn't get bigger than this. 🎾 #Wimbledon

article.headline: Sinner vs. Zverev: The Wimbledon Final That Has Everything

stories.title: Arthur Fery's Tournament Impact — Career Transformed by Wimbledon Run

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
Arthur Fery came to Wimbledon ranked No. 114.

He leaves ranked No. 36 (career high), British No. 1, with ~$1.2M in prize money — more than every dollar he earned before this tournament.

23 years old. What comes next? #Wimbledon #Fery #TennisFanrecap

facebook.long_form:
Let's take a moment to appreciate what Arthur Fery just did.

He came to Wimbledon ranked No. 114 in the world, with a wild card and a dream. Two weeks later, he's No. 36 — a career high. He's the new British No. 1, displacing Cam Norrie. The prize money from this Wimbledon run? Approximately $1.2 million. His total career prize money before this tournament? $884,000.

He entered Wimbledon having earned less than a million dollars in his entire career. He leaves having more than doubled that in 14 days.

Fery is 23, and he became only the second man in the Open Era to reach the Wimbledon semifinals as a wild card. The first was Goran Ivanisevic in 2001. Ivanisevic won that title. The bar for wild card Wimbledon performances has always had exactly one occupant — until Fery took a spot this fortnight.

He lost to Zverev 7-6(0), 6-2, 6-4 on Friday. A clinical opponent, a clean scoreline. But nothing about this tournament diminishes what Fery did to get there.

He is going to be a fixture at Grand Slams for years to come. He's already changed his life and his career.

What's next for Arthur Fery? Drop your prediction in the comments. 🎾
facebook.image_caption: No. 114 to No. 36. $884K career earnings to $1.2M in prize money in two weeks. Arthur Fery's Wimbledon changed everything. 🎾 #Wimbledon
